Firm costs + VAT = £2,880 (transferred to office account). Total from client account: £288,000 + £9,600 + £270 + £2,880 = £300,750. But wait — the firm costs are paid from client to office account (not to a third party). The amount leaving the client bank account is £288,000 (to seller) + £9,600 (to HMRC SDLT) + £270 (to HMLR) = £298,750. The £2,880 moves to office account as a client-to-office transfer rather than leaving the firm entirely. Total outgoing from client account to external parties: £298,750.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Pat Hollowell asks Crane & Pelletier to receive £200,000 into client account and hold it 'for safekeeping' while she considers how to invest it. There is no legal matter involved. What should the firm do?

options.A: Accept — holding money for clients is a core service for solicitors
options.B: Accept — the money is safe in a regulated client account
options.C: Decline — SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 3.3 prohibits firms from using client account to provide banking facilities to clients where there is no legitimate legal service being provided
options.D: Accept but charge a fee to make the arrangement a legitimate legal service

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 3.3 explicitly prohibits firms from using their client account to provide banking facilities to clients (or third parties) where there is no legitimate underlying legal matter. Holding £200,000 'for safekeeping' with no associated legal work is using the client account as a bank — this is prohibited regardless of the client's reason. Accepting such deposits exposes the firm to money laundering risk and regulatory sanction. The firm should decline and advise Pat to use a regulated bank or investment firm for safekeeping purposes.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Mixed Receipt — Office and Client Money
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier receives a single bank transfer of £7,500 from Alek Kowalski comprising £5,000 billed fees (office money) and £2,500 on account for anticipated disbursements (client money). The payment arrives as a single transfer. Which approach is compliant?

options.A: Pay the full £7,500 into office account and later transfer £2,500 to client account
options.B: Pay £5,000 into office account and £2,500 into client account simultaneously (split at receipt)
options.C: Ask Alek to send two separate transfers — the Rules prohibit single transfers covering both types of money
options.D: Pay the full £7,500 into client account; transfer £5,000 to office account promptly after receipt; retain £2,500 in client account

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 4.1 addresses mixed receipts. Where a single payment contains both office money and client money, the firm has two options: (1) split the payment at receipt — the office element to office account, the client element to client account; or (2) pay the entire mixed receipt into client account and transfer the office element (earned fees) out promptly afterwards. Option B (simultaneous split) is also compliant. Option D is correct and the more common practice. Option A (paying client money into office account) is not permissible — client money cannot go into office account even temporarily.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Ledger — Service Charge and Ground Rent
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier acts for Clive Dring buying a leasehold flat. On completion, the firm is required to pay the apportioned ground rent of £150 and service charge of £850 to the management company from the funds held. Where should these payments come from and how are they recorded?

options.A: Both payments are disbursements paid from client account: DR Clive client ledger (client side) £1,000; CR Cash (client) £1,000
options.B: Ground rent is paid from office account; service charge from client account
options.C: Both are paid from office account as they are the firm's liability to the management company
options.D: Both are deducted from the purchase price before it is paid to the seller

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Apportioned ground rent and service charge on a leasehold purchase are disbursements — payments made on behalf of the client as part of the transaction. They are paid from client account (from the completion funds held for Clive). The ledger entries record the payments out of client account: DR Clive Dring client ledger (client side) £1,000 (reducing the firm's liability to Clive); CR Cash (client) £1,000 (reducing the client bank balance). These are not the firm's liabilities — they are the client's obligations under the lease being transferred, paid by the firm on the client's behalf.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Client Account — Firm Cannot Charge Bank Charges to Client Account
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier's bank charges the firm £45 for a CHAPS transfer made from client account on behalf of Hassan Nazari. The firm wishes to debit the £45 from Hassan's client ledger. Is this permissible?

options.A: Yes — the bank charge is a disbursement incurred on Hassan's behalf and can be paid from client account
options.B: Yes — firms can charge all bank fees directly to client account
options.C: No — bank charges on the client account are office costs; they must be paid from office account and then recovered via the firm's bill, not taken directly from client account
options.D: Yes — provided the charge is under £100 and the client has been notified

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Bank charges on the client account are the firm's own operating costs — they are not disbursements made on behalf of the client. Taking bank charges directly from client account would be using client money to pay the firm's own expenses — a breach of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 (treating client money as if it were office money). The firm must pay bank charges from office account and may recover them from the client as part of the firm's costs on the bill. The distinction is that a CHAPS fee is the bank's charge to the firm for maintaining and using the client account — it is not a client disbursement.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Third Party Overpayment — Return of Funds
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: The buyer's solicitors accidentally send £400,000 instead of £360,000 to Crane & Pelletier's client account on completion of a sale for Victoria Ashworth. The firm spots the error immediately. What should the firm do with the £40,000 overpayment?

options.A: Return the £40,000 from office account and keep the £40,000 client money as a windfall
options.B: Retain the full £400,000 and ask Victoria to instruct what to do with the surplus
options.C: Return the £40,000 to the buyer's solicitors from client account promptly, as it was received in error and does not belong to Victoria or the firm
options.D: Transfer the £40,000 to the designated client account pending the buyer's solicitors' written request

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The £40,000 overpayment is not Victoria's money — it was sent in error by the buyer's solicitors. The firm has no entitlement to retain it and it would be wrong to treat it as client money belonging to Victoria (who is only entitled to the agreed sale proceeds). The firm should contact the buyer's solicitors to confirm the error and return the £40,000 from client account promptly. Retaining money received in error amounts to unjust enrichment and would breach rule 1.2 of the SRA Accounts Rules (firm must not retain client money it is not entitled to hold). All of this should be documented.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: End of Matter — Closing the Ledger
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: After completing James Ashworth's litigation matter and distributing all funds, Crane & Pelletier notices a credit balance of £22 remaining in client account on James's ledger. James cannot be located by normal means. What are the firm's obligations?

options.A: Transfer the £22 to office account as the matter is now closed
options.B: Write off the £22 as a minor administrative amount
options.C: Make reasonable efforts to return the £22 to James; if unsuccessful after a reasonable period, comply with the SRA's process for residual balances under rule 2.5, which may involve SRA approval to withdraw and donate to charity
options.D: Hold the £22 in client account indefinitely without further action

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 2.5 of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 specifically addresses residual balances. A firm cannot simply transfer unclaimed client money to office account — it remains the client's money. The firm must: (1) take reasonable steps to return the money to the client (letters, searches, Companies House checks); (2) document all steps taken; and (3) if unable to return the money after a reasonable time, contact the SRA. The SRA's guidance allows firms to close a residual balance to office account or donate to charity in certain circumstances, but this requires following the approved process — not unilateral action. The amount being small (£22) does not change the obligation.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier holds £30,000 for Hollowell Engineering in client account. Hollowell Engineering enters administration. The administrator demands the money be released to the administrator. Must the firm comply?

options.A: No — client account money is protected and cannot be claimed by administrators
options.B: Yes — an administrator has authority over all company assets including money held by solicitors on behalf of the company
options.C: The firm should comply with the administrator's demand once identity and authority are verified — the client money belongs to the company and the administrator steps into the company's shoes
options.D: The firm must pay into court pending resolution of the administration

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Client money held by a solicitor belongs beneficially to the client (Hollowell Engineering). On administration, the administrator takes control of the company's assets on behalf of the creditors. The £30,000 is a company asset — it has not been separated from the company's estate by any trust mechanism specific to it. Once the firm verifies the administrator's identity and authority (Insolvency Act 1986 Schedule B1), the firm must comply with the administrator's lawful instructions and release the money. The client account protection means the money is not at risk from the firm's insolvency — not from the client's own insolvency.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier receives the proceeds of a civil fraud settlement (£500,000) for Clive Dring into client account. After the settlement, it becomes apparent the settlement proceeds represent the proceeds of Clive's own fraud against a third party. What is the firm's exposure?

options.A: The firm has no liability — it received the money innocently
options.B: The firm's liability is limited to regulatory sanction — no criminal exposure
options.C: The firm must immediately return the money to the third party victim
options.D: The firm may have committed money laundering offences under POCA 2002 ss.327-329 by receiving the proceeds of crime; the firm must consider whether it had knowledge or suspicion and whether a SAR is required

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: POCA 2002 s.327 (concealing/disguising criminal property), s.328 (arrangements facilitating acquisition of criminal property), and s.329 (acquiring criminal property) can apply to solicitors who receive or deal with the proceeds of crime. If the firm had knowledge or suspicion that the settlement money represented proceeds of Clive's fraud, receiving it into client account could constitute a money laundering offence. The firm must: (1) consider the extent of its knowledge at the time; (2) consider whether consent was obtained from the NCA before the receipt; (3) consider whether a retrospective SAR is required; and (4) take specialist advice. The 'tipping off' prohibition applies throughout.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Funds Received Before Exchange — Holding as Stakeholder
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: On a residential sale, the buyer's solicitors send a 10% deposit of £25,000 to Crane & Pelletier (acting for the seller) to be held as stakeholder. Exchange takes place. The transaction then fails due to the buyer's breach. The seller wishes to forfeit the deposit. Can the firm release it to the seller immediately?

options.A: Yes — as stakeholder for the seller, the firm can release funds to the seller on breach by the buyer
options.B: Yes — the seller's solicitor has authority to instruct the firm to release on forfeiture
options.C: Yes — once exchange has taken place, the deposit automatically belongs to the seller
options.D: No — a stakeholder holds for both parties; the firm cannot release the deposit to the seller without the buyer's written agreement or a court order, even where the seller claims the buyer is in breach

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: A stakeholder holds money neutrally for both parties — neither party has an immediate entitlement to require payment until either (1) both agree; or (2) the court adjudicates. Even where the seller argues the buyer is in breach (entitling the seller to forfeit the deposit under the Standard Conditions of Sale), the stakeholder cannot act on that claim unilaterally. The buyer may dispute the alleged breach. Releasing the funds without agreement or court order would expose the firm to liability to the buyer if the forfeiture is later found to be wrongful. The firm must await agreement or seek a court order.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Ledger — Acting for Lender and Buyer: Split Billing
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier acts for both Northgate Couriers (buyer) and its mortgage lender on a commercial property purchase. The firm charges Northgate £6,000 + VAT (£1,200) for the whole transaction but the lender's portion of the work is worth £1,200 + VAT (£240). How should the costs be allocated in the ledgers?

options.A: Charge the full £7,200 to Northgate's ledger — the lender pays nothing
options.B: Split the total bill equally between Northgate and the lender
options.C: Charge the full £7,200 to the lender as it is benefiting most from the transaction
options.D: Raise two bills: £4,800 + VAT £960 to Northgate; £1,200 + VAT £240 to the lender — each client is billed for the work attributable to them, with separate office ledger entries for each

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Where a firm acts for two clients in the same transaction, each client is a separate client with a separate ledger. The firm must bill each client for the work done specifically for them. The lender's work (reviewing title, approving the mortgage, requisitions on title) is separate from the buyer's work — and the lender typically has a separate engagement. The lender's bill (£1,440 including VAT) is recorded: DR Lender office ledger £1,440; CR Office costs £1,200; CR VAT £240. Northgate's bill (£5,760 including VAT) is similarly recorded on its own ledger. Each client should receive a separate bill clearly showing what they are being charged for.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier receives a fee note from counsel for £3,500 + VAT (£700) for a hearing. The firm is acting as agent for the client and pays from client account. The firm then includes this as a disbursement on the client's bill. Is the VAT on counsel's fee recoverable as input tax by Crane & Pelletier?

options.A: Yes — all VAT on disbursements paid by solicitors is recoverable as input tax
options.B: No — counsel's fees are exempt from VAT and no VAT is charged
options.C: Yes — the firm recovers the VAT and bills the client only the net fee of £3,500
options.D: No — when the firm acts as agent for the client in paying counsel's fees, the VAT is the client's input tax (if registered) or irrecoverable cost; the firm passes the full £4,200 through as a disbursement and does not recover the VAT itself

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Where a solicitor acts as agent for the client in instructing counsel (the client is the principal), the VAT on counsel's fee is the client's liability — not the firm's. The firm pays the full £4,200 out of client account and passes it through as a disbursement (£4,200) on the bill. The firm does not claim input tax recovery on agency disbursements. If the client is VAT-registered, the client may recover the VAT on their own return. If the firm were the principal (instructing counsel on its own account), it could recover input tax. The correct VAT treatment depends entirely on the agency/principal analysis.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Clive Dring loses £40,000 of his savings when a dishonest fee earner at Crane & Pelletier steals client money. The firm is insolvent. What is Clive's remedy?

options.A: Clive has no remedy as the firm is insolvent
options.B: The SRA Compensation Fund is only available for losses caused by the firm's own insolvency, not dishonesty
options.C: Clive must claim against the firm's professional indemnity insurance only
options.D: Clive can claim from the SRA Compensation Fund, which can award compensation for losses caused by solicitors' dishonesty or failure to account for client money

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The SRA Compensation Fund (formerly the Solicitors Compensation Fund) provides a safety net for those who suffer loss as a result of dishonesty by a solicitor or their employees, or a failure to account for client money. Under the SRA Compensation Fund Rules, eligible claimants can receive grants from the Fund where the solicitor's firm is unable to make good the loss (e.g. due to insolvency). The maximum grant per claim can be substantial — the Fund compensates genuine victims of dishonesty. Professional indemnity insurance (PI) is the primary source for negligence claims; the Compensation Fund covers dishonesty where PI is exhausted or inapplicable.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: The SRA intervenes in Crane & Pelletier following the discovery of widespread misappropriation of client funds. What happens to client files and money on intervention?

options.A: Client files are destroyed and money is returned to clients immediately
options.B: The firm's partners are personally liable to return all client money immediately
options.C: A receiver is appointed by the court to manage the distribution
options.D: The SRA takes control of the practice; client files and money vest in the SRA; the SRA arranges for files to be transferred and money to be returned to clients or held securely

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Under Schedule 1 to the Solicitors Act 1974, on intervention the SRA takes control of the practice. Client money vests in the SRA and is held for clients. The SRA arranges: (1) for client files and papers to be transferred to new solicitors appointed by the clients; (2) for client money to be accounted for and returned; and (3) for the Compensation Fund to be available to compensate those who have suffered loss. Intervention is a drastic step — the SRA uses it where the solicitor is dishonest, incapacitated, or where there is a risk to the public or client money.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: The Reporting Accountant for Crane & Pelletier discovers during the annual review that the firm paid office expenses from client account on three occasions totalling £1,800, all of which have been corrected. Must the accountant qualify the report?

options.A: No — corrected breaches do not need to be reported
options.B: No — the accountant only qualifies if the breach is ongoing at the date of the report
options.C: Yes — under rule 12, a qualified report is required where the accountant finds a material breach of the SRA Accounts Rules, even if corrected; misuse of client account (however corrected) is a material breach
options.D: No — the total of £1,800 is below the materiality threshold of £5,000

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Under SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 12, the Reporting Accountant must qualify the report if they have found a material breach of the Rules (or breaches that, taken together, are material) during the period — even if corrected before the report is prepared. Using client account to pay office expenses three times totalling £1,800 is a material breach (misuse of client money) regardless of correction. A qualified report must be delivered to the SRA. There is no monetary materiality threshold specified in the Rules — materiality is assessed in context. The SRA then follows up with the firm. Correction is a mitigating factor but not a reason to issue an unqualified report.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Client Account — Receipt of Indemnity Insurance Payment
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier receives £15,000 from a third-party insurer representing indemnity for loss suffered by Diana Pelletier in a property transaction. The cheque is payable to Crane & Pelletier as trustee for Diana. What is the correct accounting treatment?

options.A: Pay into office account — it is a firm receipt from the insurer
options.B: Pay into office account — indemnity payments are not client money
options.C: Pay into a designated account in the insurer's name pending confirmation of entitlement
options.D: Pay into client account — the money is received on behalf of Diana as client money and the firm holds it on trust for her

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Money received on behalf of a client in connection with legal services is client money under SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 2.1. The indemnity payment is received by Crane & Pelletier on behalf of Diana — the cheque is payable to the firm as trustee for Diana, confirming she is the beneficial owner. It must be paid into client account: DR Cash (client) £15,000; CR Diana Pelletier client ledger (client side) £15,000. The firm then distributes it to Diana in accordance with her instructions (less any outstanding costs, once billed). Paying it into office account would be a misappropriation of client money.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Retention of Funds Pending Resolution of Dispute
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: After completing Hollowell Engineering's commercial lease transaction, a dispute arises between Hollowell Engineering and the landlord about a deposit. Crane & Pelletier holds £10,000 in client account that both parties claim. What should the firm do with the £10,000 while the dispute is unresolved?

options.A: Pay the £10,000 to Hollowell Engineering as it is the firm's client
options.B: Pay the £10,000 to the landlord to avoid conflict
options.C: Transfer the £10,000 to office account until the dispute is resolved
options.D: Retain the £10,000 in client account, notify both parties that the funds are held pending resolution, and obtain their agreement or a court order before releasing — consider paying into court if no resolution is reached

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Where a solicitor holds money that is the subject of a dispute between parties, the firm must not release the funds to either party without (1) agreement from both parties; or (2) a court order. The money remains in client account — it cannot be transferred to office account (which would be misappropriation). The firm should notify both parties clearly that the funds are held, that they will not be released without resolution, and suggest they seek agreement or apply to court. If the position becomes untenable, the firm may consider paying the funds into court for the court to adjudicate the claim. Acting unilaterally in favour of either party would be a serious breach.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: General Client Account vs Designated Client Account — Interest
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier holds £100,000 for Ashworth Developments Ltd in its general client account for 6 months. Interest earned on the general client account pool is £3,200 but only £800 is attributable to Ashworth's funds. The firm pays Ashworth £800. Is this compliant?

options.A: Yes — the firm pays interest attributable to the specific client's funds and keeps the rest
options.B: No — the firm must pay Ashworth the full £3,200 pool interest
options.C: No — the firm must open a designated account for any amount over £50,000
options.D: Yes — rule 7 requires only that the amount paid is a 'fair sum', which may be calculated on the firm's own methodology provided it reflects the client's funds and time held

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 7 requires firms to pay clients a fair sum in lieu of interest, or to account for actual interest. The firm is not required to account for the exact pro-rata share of interest earned on the pool if it has a fair methodology — it can use a calculated rate applied to the amount held and the time period. If £800 represents a fair calculation of what Ashworth would have earned (applying reasonable rates), this is compliant. The firm retains the difference as part of the float benefit of operating a pooled account. However, the firm's interest policy must be fair and the calculation transparent. There is no fixed £50,000 threshold in the Rules for requiring a designated account.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Authorised Withdrawals from Client Account — Rule 5
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Which of the following is NOT a permitted reason to withdraw money from a client account under SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 5?

options.A: To pay a sum of money to or on behalf of the client
options.B: To pay a disbursement on behalf of the client
options.C: To transfer earned costs to office account after a bill has been delivered
options.D: To fund the firm's general operating expenses because the office account is temporarily overdrawn

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 5.1 of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 sets out the exhaustive list of permitted withdrawals from client account: (a) to make a payment to or on behalf of the client; (b) to carry out the client's instructions; (c) to pay a disbursement on behalf of the client; (d) in satisfaction of a firm's costs where a bill has been given or notification of costs has been provided; and (e) to reimburse the firm for money it has paid out of its own funds on behalf of the client. Funding the firm's general operating expenses is not a permitted purpose — this would constitute misuse of client money, a serious breach of the Rules regardless of the firm's financial position or intention to repay.
